content:
From Monolith to Microservices: A Practical Modernization Journey with .NET, Modular Monoliths, Strangler Gateway, RabbitMQ, and Observability

10 min read
2 hours ago

--

Modernizing a legacy application is one of the most misunderstood topics in software architecture.
Many teams hear the word “modernization” and immediately think of microservices, Kubernetes, cloud-native platforms, event-driven architecture, and distributed tracing. Those things can be useful, but jumping directly from a monolith to microservices is often the fastest way to create a distributed monolith, operational complexity, and a very tired engineering team.
Real modernization is not a rewrite.

Free Link:- In case if your medium quota is expired. Read it for free here.
Github Link:- https://github.com/rahulsahay19/Modernization-Playbook
Subscribe here:- Launch Offer
Real modernization is a sequence of careful, business-aware engineering steps.
In this article, I want to walk through a practical modernization journey using a healthcare claims processing system as the example. We will start with a traditional monolith, evolve it into a modular monolith, introduce a Strangler Fig gateway, extract the first real microservice, add reliable messaging with the outbox pattern and RabbitMQ, and finally observe the full request flow using Aspire Dashboard and OpenTelemetry.
The stack used in this journey is:
.NET / ASP.NET Core
React
Modular Monolith
Clean Architecture
CQRS without MediatR
Strangler Fig Pattern
RabbitMQ
Outbox Pattern
SQLite
OpenTelemetry
Aspire Dashboard
The goal is not to chase architecture buzzwords.
The goal is to reduce risk while moving toward a system that is easier to understand, change, scale, and operate.
The Starting Point: A Healthcare Claims Monolith
Our starting application is a healthcare claims platform.
It includes capabilities such as:
Policies
Members
Provider Network
Claims
Documents
Payments
Notifications
Audit
Reporting
In the beginning, everything lives inside one ASP.NET Core application.
One API.
One deployable.
One process.
One database.
A simplified flow looks like this:
React Portal -> Monolith API -> Shared Database
Subscribe here:- Launch Offer
This kind of architecture is not automatically bad. In fact, many successful products start this way because a monolith is simple to build, simple to deploy, and simple to reason about when the business is still evolving.
The problem usually appears over time.
As more features are added, the boundaries become blurry. Claims logic starts calling document logic directly. Payments update claim state. Notifications are triggered from deep inside workflows. Reporting reads from everywhere. A change in one area can unexpectedly break another.
The architecture becomes less about business capabilities and more about shared technical convenience.
That is where modernization pressure starts.
Why Not Jump Directly to Microservices?
A common mistake is to look at a large monolith and say:
“Let’s break this into microservices.”
That sounds attractive, but it often skips the most important question:
“Do we understand the boundaries well enough to split them?”
If the boundaries are not clear inside a single process, they will not magically become clear when distributed across many processes.
Instead, you may end up with:
Too many services
Chatty synchronous calls
Shared database dependencies
Duplicated business rules
Harder debugging
Harder deployments
Operational overhead
Distributed transactions
Microservices are not a cleanup strategy.
They are a deployment and ownership strategy that works best after boundaries are understood.
That is why the first real step in this journey is not microservices.
It is modularity.
Step 1: Move from Monolith to Modular Monolith
A modular monolith keeps one deployable application, but introduces clear internal boundaries.
The system still runs as one ASP.NET Core process, but the code is organized around business capabilities:
Claims Module
Policy Module
Membership Module
Provider Network Module
Documents Module
Payments Module
Communications Module
Audit Module
Reporting Module
The runtime is still simple:
React Portal -> Modular Monolith API -> Modules
But internally, the architecture is much healthier.
Each module owns its own responsibilities. The Claims module should not directly reach into the Documents module’s repositories. Payments should not directly mutate Claims internals. Reporting should not become a backdoor into every module’s private data.
The key rule is:
Modules should not depend directly on other modules.
Instead, communication happens through explicit contracts and events.
This gives us many benefits without introducing distributed-system complexity:
Clearer ownership
Better code organization
Easier testing
Reduced accidental coupling
A safer foundation for future extraction
This stage is extremely important because it forces us to discover business boundaries before we distribute them. A modular monolith is not a failure to reach microservices. A modular monolith is often the safest bridge toward microservices.
Subscribe here:- Launch Offer
Step 2: Keep the Frontend Stable
In many modernization projects, the frontend becomes a casualty of backend restructuring. That is a mistake. In this journey, the React portal is intentionally kept stable. It continues to call business APIs like:
/api/claims
/api/documents
/api/payments
/api/reports/operations
The frontend should not need to know whether /api/documents is served by the monolith, modular monolith, or an extracted microservice. That separation is powerful. It allows us to modernize backend ownership without rewriting the user experience. At different stages, the React portal can point to different backends:
Monolith
Modular Monolith
Gateway
The frontend remains focused on claims operations. The backend architecture evolves behind the API boundary. That is a very practical modernization principle:
Modernize the backend without destabilizing the user experience.
Step 3: Introduce the Strangler Gateway
Once the modular monolith is in place, the next step is introducing a Strangler Fig gateway. Initially, the flow looks like this:
React Portal -> Strangler Gateway -> Modular Monolith
The gateway becomes the stable entry point for frontend API calls. The frontend still calls:
/api/*But instead of calling the modular monolith directly, requests go through the gateway. The gateway has a route table that decides which backend owns each capability. For example:
{
"RouteId": "documents",
"Capability": "Documents",
"PathPrefix": "/api/documents",
"UpstreamName": "DocumentsService",
"DestinationBaseUrl": "http://localhost:5240",
"Extracted": true
}This is the key Strangler Fig idea:
Keep the external API stable.
Move internal ownership capability by capability.
At first, all routes may still go to the modular monolith. Later, one capability moves out. Then another. Then another. The gateway lets us migrate gradually instead of rewriting the entire system. After the Documents capability is extracted, the flow becomes:
The frontend does not need to change. The route ownership changes behind the gateway. That is the strangler pattern in action.
Step 4: Extract the First Microservice
In this journey, Documents is the first extracted service. Why Documents?Because document handling often has a clear boundary:
Upload document metadata
List documents
Verify document
Reject document
Track document status
It is related to claims, but it does not need to live inside the same module forever. So we extract it into a separate service:
microservices/documents-service
The service is structured using clean architecture:
DocumentsService.Api
Endpoints
Program.cs
DocumentsService.Application
Commands
Queries
Handlers
DTOs
Abstractions
IntegrationEvents
DocumentsService.Domain
Entities
Enums
DocumentsService.Infrastructure
Persistence
Repositories
IntegrationEvents
This separation keeps responsibilities clear.
The API layer handles HTTP.
The Application layer handles use cases.
The Domain layer contains business entities and enums.
The Infrastructure layer deals with database, messaging, and external concerns.
The Documents service also follows CQRS, but without MediatR.
That means we still separate commands and queries:
Subscribe here:- Launch Offer
Step 5: Give the Service Its Own Database
Once Documents becomes a microservice, it should own its data.
So the Documents service uses its own SQLite database:
.local-data/documents-service/documents.dbThis matters because a service boundary is not just about code. A real service boundary includes data ownership. If the extracted service still writes directly into the monolith’s database, then we have not really extracted the capability. We have only moved code into another process while keeping the deepest coupling in place.
The better direction is:
Documents Service -> Own Database
Modular Monolith -> Own internal state
Of course, this creates a new question. If the Documents service owns document data, how does the modular monolith know that a document was registered, verified, or rejected? That is where integration events come in.
Step 6: Introduce Integration Events
When a document is registered, the Documents service publishes an integration event:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

DocumentRegisteredIntegrationEvent
When a document is verified:
DocumentVerifiedIntegrationEvent
When a document is rejected:
DocumentRejectedIntegrationEvent
These events cross the service boundary. The modular monolith receives them and converts them into internal business events:
DocumentRegisteredEvent
DocumentVerifiedEvent
DocumentRejectedEvent
This distinction is important:
Integration events cross service boundaries.
Business events stay inside a process.
The Documents service should not directly call the modular monolith’s Audit module.
It should publish an event that says:
“A document was verified.”
The modular monolith can then decide what to do internally:
Update audit trail
Create notification
Update operational reporting
This removes runtime coupling. The Documents service does not need to know who is listening.
Step 7: Add the Outbox Pattern
Publishing events directly inside a request handler can be risky.
Imagine this flow:
Save document to database
Publish event to RabbitMQ
What happens if the document is saved successfully, but RabbitMQ is temporarily unavailable?
Now the system has inconsistent state.
The document exists, but the event was never published.
The outbox pattern solves this problem.
Instead of publishing immediately, the service writes the event into an outbox table in the same database transaction as the business change.
The flow becomes:
Save document
Save outbox message
Background worker publishes pending outbox messages
Mark message as published
This gives us a more reliable event publishing model. In our Documents service:
Documents Service
-> SQLite document row
-> SQLite outbox row
-> OutboxPublisherBackgroundService
-> RabbitMQ
The important teaching point is:
Business state and event intent are stored together.
Publishing can happen safely in the background.
This is one of the most practical patterns in distributed systems.
Subscribe here:- Launch Offer
Step 8: Replace Local Relay with RabbitMQ
Earlier in the journey, events can be relayed through simple HTTP endpoints or a local file broker. But in a more realistic distributed system, we introduce a message broker. Here, we use RabbitMQ.
The final broker setup is:
Exchange: claims.integration.events
Routing key: documents.events
Queue: modular-monolith.documents
The flow becomes:
Documents Service
-> Outbox
-> RabbitMQ Exchange
-> RabbitMQ Queue
-> Modular Monolith Consumer
The modular monolith runs a RabbitMQ consumer. When it receives a brokered document event, it dispatches it internally:
BrokeredIntegrationEvent
-> DocumentIntegrationEventDispatcher
-> Internal Business Event
-> In-process Event Bus
-> Audit / Communi
